questions.question: In an Angular NgModule, what is the difference between 'declarations' and 'imports'?
options: declarations list services; imports list components
declarations list components/directives/pipes owned by this module; imports list other modules whose exports this module needs
declarations list what to export publicly; imports list what to keep private
There is no difference — both accept components and modules interchangeably

questions.question: In Spring Boot, what is the purpose of @Configuration on a class?
options: It marks the class as the application entry point (like main class)
It tells Spring this class contains @Bean methods that define objects to manage
It activates component scanning in the package
It imports external Spring modules similar to Angular's imports array

questions.question: What is the key advantage of Angular standalone components over NgModule-based components?
options: Standalone components run faster at runtime
Standalone components can use @Output without @Input
Standalone components declare their own imports directly, eliminating the need for a separate module file
Standalone components automatically provide HttpClient without provideHttpClient()

questions.question: If UserService is listed in UserModule's providers array (not providedIn: 'root'), what is the scope of that service?
options: Application-wide singleton shared across all modules
A new instance is created per component that injects it
A separate instance is created for each lazy-loaded module that imports UserModule
The service is only available in unit tests

questions.question: Spring Boot auto-configuration activates @Configuration classes based on what?
options: The order of beans declared in application.properties
What's present on the classpath — starter dependencies trigger their matching configuration
Explicit @Import annotations in your main class
The package name matching a Spring naming convention
